Isaiah 3 ends with Zion stripped bare—her women left without husbands, her pride consumed, her streets filled with violence. It is a landscape of catastrophe. But Isaiah 4 refuses to let that devastation have the final word. Instead, it opens with a vision so startling that it almost defies comprehension: seven women—a number signifying completeness—will desperately cling to a single man, willing to surrender everything except the right to bear his name. This is not romance. This is survival. In their degradation, they beg only to be called by his name and to have their shame removed. The text does not soften what has happened. It acknowledges the weight of loss—these women have lost husbands, security, and dignity. Yet even in their brokenness, they cling to identity, to belonging, to being claimed by someone. God sees this desperation and does not turn away.

What follows in verse 2 is the pivot that makes sense of everything: "On that day the Branch of the LORD will be beautiful and glorious." That "day" is not some distant, abstract future—it is the day when God acts to restore. The Branch is Christ, the royal descendant who will emerge from the line of David, who will embody beauty and glory that the world cannot diminish. In Him, the survivors of judgment—those who remain in Zion, those recorded among the living—will be called holy. Not by their own virtue or achievement, but by His presence and work among them. This is the movement of Scripture: judgment precedes restoration, and restoration comes only through the promised One.

But what does holiness actually look like in practice? It comes through cleansing—the Lord washing away filth, burning away bloodstains, using both judgment and fire to purify. These are not gentle metaphors. They describe God's relentless work to separate His people from everything that corrupts them. The image is visceral: blood washed away, filth removed, the very heart of Jerusalem refined by the heat of divine judgment. Only after this cleansing can the people be called holy. Only after devastation can they be restored.

The chapter closes with an image of extraordinary tenderness: God will create a protective presence over Mount Zion—a cloud by day, a flame by night, echoing the wilderness wandering when God's glory led Israel forward. This is not merely comfort; it is covenant presence. The shelter, the shade, the hiding place—all provided by God Himself—speak to His willingness to shield His people not from consequences, but in the midst of restoration. Those women who once had nothing but shame will find themselves under the canopy of His glory. That is what awaits those who are His. Not escape from judgment, but deliverance through it.
